wiki_pages.title: Job Platforms & Networking Resources
wiki_pages.content_html:
Resources:

Be sure to check out AnnieCannons' [AnnieCannons JOB PLATFORMS ], which includes:

  • Online job platforms

  • Professional networking platforms

  • Additional resources for job seekers

Feel free to make a copy if you'd like to edit or customize it!

Reminder:

Tech communities and forums are excellent ways to engage with like-minded professionals. They offer opportunities to:

  • Learn from others

  • Seek guidance on technical topics

  • Stay updated on industry trends and best practices

Consider joining platforms such as GitHub, Dev.to, Stack Overflow, or relevant Discord/Slack communities to expand your professional network!

wiki_pages.title: Frontend Mentor projects explained
wiki_pages.content_html:
These challenges are for you if you have completed all of your assignments. (Don't forget to review those incompletes!)

We have downloaded and organized these Frontend Mentor projects for you, which are appropriate to your level of learning.

Submit a Rating (Newbie level)

Your challenge is to build out this interactive rating component and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

Your users should be able to:

  • Select and submit a number rating
  • See the "Thank you" card state after submitting a rating
  • See hover and focus states for all interactive elements on the page

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first

 

Pod request access landing page (Newbie level)

Your challenge is to build out this early access landing page and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

This challenge does include some JS, but it's only a small amount for the custom form validation.

Your users should be able to:

  • Receive an error message when the form is submitted if:
    • The Email address field is empty should show "Oops! Please add your email"
    • The email is not formatted correctly should show "Oops! Please check your email"
  • View the optimal layout depending on their device's screen size
  • See hover states for interactive elements

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first

 

Character counter/text analyzer (Junior level)

Your challenge is to build out this character counter and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

Your users should be able to:

  • Analyze the character, word, and sentence counts for their text
  • Exclude/Include spaces in their character count
  • Set a character limit
  • Receive a warning message if their text exceeds their character limit
  • See the approximate reading time of their text
  • Analyze the letter density of their text
  • Select their color theme
  • Navigate the app and perform all actions using only their keyboard
  • View the optimal layout for the interface depending on their device's screen size
  • See hover and focus states for all interactive elements on the page

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first

 

Mortgage Repayment Calculator (Junior level)

Your challenge is to build out this mortgage repayment calculator and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

Your users should be able to:

  • Input mortgage information and see monthly repayment and total repayment amounts after submitting the form
  • See form validation messages if any field is incomplete
  • Complete the form only using their keyboard
  • View the optimal layout for the interface depending on their device's screen size
  • See hover and focus states for all interactive elements on the page

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first


Age Calculator App (Junior level)

Your challenge is to build out this age calculator app and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

  • View an age in years, months, and days after submitting a valid date through the form
  • Receive validation errors if:
    • Any field is empty when the form is submitted
    • The day number is not between 1-31
    • The month number is not between 1-12
    • The date is in the future
    • The date is invalid, e.g. 31/04/1991 (there are 30 days in April)
  • View the optimal layout for the interface depending on their device's screen size
  • See hover and focus states for all interactive elements on the page
  • Bonus: See the age numbers animate to their final number when the form is submitted

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first

 

Tip Calculator + Check Splitter (Junior level)

Your challenge is to build out this tip calculator app and get it looking as close to the design as possible.

You can use any tools you like to help you complete the challenge. So if you've got something you'd like to practice, feel free to give it a go.

Your users should be able to:

  • Calculate the correct tip and total cost of the bill per person
  • View the optimal layout for the app depending on their device's screen size
  • See hover states for all interactive elements on the page

Download the project and go through the README.md file. This will provide further details about the project and help you get set up.

Get started

  • Download starter files as a zip
  • Download Figma file (optional)
  • Pseudocode first

wiki_pages.title: Technical Interview Prep Resources & Planning Copy
wiki_pages.content_html:
Technical Interview Prep ResourcesLinks to an external site.

Remember that these resources and tools can support your job search and career development in the tech sector. It's important to explore and utilize those that align with your specific goals and interests. In preparation as you apply for roles, remember to ask the recruiter/person of contact what type of prep should be done and what the technical questions will focus on.
Always stay curious, keep learning, and leverage these resources to enhance your technical competence and interview performance. Good luck!

REMEMBER THAT NOTHING NEEDS TO BE PAID FOR—ALL RESOURCES ARE FREE!

wiki_pages.title: 🌟 Job Board Filter Guide for Software Development Roles
wiki_pages.content_html:
🌟 Job Board Filter Guide for Software Development Roles

(Front-End, Back-End, Full-Stack)

Support for career changers, bootcamp grads, reentry professionals, and others from nontraditional paths.

✅ Filters to Use for More Relevant, Supportive Roles

1. Experience Level

  • Entry-Level, Internship, Associate, Apprenticeship

  • Apprenticeships: paid, structured, mentorship-based learning roles

  • Avoid Mid/Senior unless explicitly open to all experience levels

2. Job Type

  • Full-Time, Part-Time, Contract, Apprenticeship

  • Freelance or Remote Internships for flexibility and skill-building

3. Location

  • Remote = broader access and equity

  • Hybrid or Onsite if seeking local in-person opportunities

4. Technologies/Keywords

Customize for your role and skills:

Front-End: "Junior Front-End Developer", React, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, Tailwind, Figma

Back-End: "Junior Back-End Developer", Node.js, Express, Python, SQL, MongoDB

Full-Stack: "Junior Full-Stack Developer", MERN, JavaScript, TypeScript, API, Git

5. Industry/Company Type

Target values-aligned or mission-driven industries:

  • EdTech

  • Climate/Sustainability

  • Healthcare/Mental Health

  • Creative/Media/Storytelling

  • Nonprofit/Social Impact

  • Civic/Government Tech

6. Fair Chance/Second Chance Employment

Supportive of:

  • Individuals with past incarceration or legal system involvement

  • Those navigating gaps due to recovery, caregiving, or other barriers

Search for "Fair Chance Employer" and "Second Chance Hiring." Use platforms like Checkr's Fair Chance Employers

7. Inclusive Practices to Prioritize

  • Mentorship and onboarding support

  • Job descriptions with learning language (not just degrees)

    • Phrases like "We welcome self-taught developers," "Relevant experience counts," "No CS degree required," and "Portfolio or bootcamp experience accepted."

    • Descriptions that highlight "growth mindset," "on-the-job learning," "mentorship provided," and "we support career changers."

  • Teams that value lived experience and diverse pathways

Use this guide alongside LinkedIn or job boards to filter with intention when building a new professional identity.

Resources:

Be sure to check out AnnieCannons' [AnnieCannons JOB PLATFORMS], which includes:

  • Online job platforms

  • Professional networking platforms

  • Additional resources for job seekers

Feel free to make a copy if you'd like to edit or customize it!
wiki_pages.manifest_title: 🌟 Job Board Filter Guide for Software Development Roles

wiki_pages.title: Bento Grid Instructions
wiki_pages.content_html:
Create the following design. You may start in CodePen, then switch to VS Code, then push your design to Github and Github Pages. Alternatively, you may start working in VS Code directly.

Mobile design: 

 

Desktop design:

All project images to download plus the mobile and desktop images shown: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1f349iqXeNRSnyWEuV6F33LZeMT8cdsZv/view?usp=sharing 

 

Suggested approach

  • Build your mobile layout first.
    • Start in CodePen if you want: https://codepen.io/team/anniecannons/pen/bGXryog 
    • Or start in VS Code directly. Download the images for the project: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1f349iqXeNRSnyWEuV6F33LZeMT8cdsZv/view?usp=sharing 
    • Set up a series of cards. Mark up the cards with the appropriate HTML and images.
    • Style each card using the appropriate colors, fonts, images. (See style guide below for details.)
    • Get your design as close as possible to the design shown above.
  • Now build the desktop layout.
    • Apply CSS Grid to make this layout. Remember to figure out how many rows and columns are needed.
    • Adjust your font size if needed.
  • Save your work to Github and Github Pages.
    • Push to Github: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DA1ema-s9oH27EsfFDmRKHc73Jc1UBI9eF4vxHqkbWU/edit?usp=sharing
    • Github to Github Pages: https://docs.google.com/document/d/13eyP13lvrz_rVQTy4SS-2GrsyWnNuJdGraOiVzc1xJs/edit?usp=sharing 

Style guide information

The designs were created to the following widths:
Mobile: 375px 
Desktop: 1440px

What breakpoints should you use? Take a a look at your card designs. Where does it look best? Set your breakpoint there to transition from mobile to desktop layout. If you want to include an intermediate tablet layout, that's a great thing to do.


Colors
Light Purple: #dacffc
Dark Purple: #7650dc
Light Yellow: #f9eee1
Gold: #ffcb6b 
White: #ffffff
Black: #121212

Fonts
Font size (paragraph): 1.125rem
Font Family: DM Sans
Weights: 400, 500

wiki_pages.title: Canvas coding snippets for instructors
wiki_pages.content_html:
After clicking Edit in the upper right corner of the editing window, in the lower right corner of the editing window, you will see a </> icon. Clicking this will put you into the HTML for each page or assignment. You may edit the HTML directly, or you may incorporate inline styles for the document.

Below are some code snippets I've used in this course. Copy the snips from this page - do not copy from source code, as it will convert brackets to > and < symbols.

Late Assignment snippet

Using the HTML <details> element, we can create an accordion panel without JavaScript!

<p> </p>
<details style="padding: 0.5rem; border-radius: 5px; background-color: #eff; border: 1px solid #cdd;">
    <summary style="font-size: 1.5rem;">Late assignments/ Incomplete Assignments</summary>
    <p><span>In the case of incomplete assignments, please note that it is crucial to provide as much information as possible. This will help us understand why you were unable to meet the deadline and how we can best support you in completing the work.</span></p>
    <p><span>In the comments for the assignment, please include the following:</span></p>
    <ol>
        <li aria-level="1"><span>Why the assignment is late</span></li>
        <li aria-level="1"><span>What you still need to do for the assignment</span></li>
        <li aria-level="1"><span>Your expected timeline for completing the work</span></li>
        <li aria-level="1"><span>If you would like to copy and paste the example here as a template please do so</span></li>
    </ol>
    <p><strong>**You must include a link in order for your submission to work. Feel free to use www.google.com.</strong></p>
    <p><span>For example:</span></p>
    <p><span> “The ________ project is not complete because _________. I have ________, ________, and ________ complete but still need more time to complete _________, _________, and _________. I estimate that I will have this done by _________________.”</span></p>
</details>

 

Canvas integrates Bootstrap 3

Documentation: https://getbootstrap.com/docs/3.3/css/#tables -- table formatting

Some, but not all, of Bootstrap 3's styles are compatible with Canvas. You will have to try them out. In general, you'll find the table formatting is good, as well as some of the color styles.

wiki_pages.title: AI Prompt library & resources
wiki_pages.content_html:
✨ How to Use This  AI Resource

Explore the shared prompts, tools, resources, and workflows others have documented to see what’s possible with AI. AI Prompt library & resources : TEMPLATE  (*make your own copy if you like)

This is a living collection of ideas to help you discover how AI can support your work. Browse examples to spark your own experiments, adapt what others have tried, and contribute back when you find something that works for you.

Remember:

  • Not every tool or workflow is right for every context—use your judgment and keep our data privacy and security policies in mind.

  • As coders and learners, AI can be a powerful aid—but it doesn’t replace foundational problem-solving or critical thinking. Use it thoughtfully to complement your skills, not shortcut them.

wiki_pages.title: What are 12 JavaScript Code Challenges for Beginners?
wiki_pages.content_html:
These 12 JavaScript Code Challenges for Beginners come from Codecademy. From the article:

Online courses are a great place to learn the foundations of code. But it’s when you put things into practice that you cement the concepts into your brain.

How to complete this work

The goal is for you to practice! Complete as many of these as you can. 

You will see 12 assignments for these challenges in Canvas. You are required to complete 3 of these. However, we encourage you to complete more. Focus Friday may be a good time to work on these.

 

Notice there is no due date for this assignment, nor is there a "late" statement to file. These problems are for you to work on if you have your other regular weekly assignments completed. You may work on these problems throughout the term.
wiki_pages.manifest_title: What are 12 JavaScript Code Challenges for Beginners?

wiki_pages.title: Week 5 Overview - More Loops + Functions + Scope
wiki_pages.content_html:
This week will:

  • Learn how to make functions dynamic
  • Learn the difference between local and global variables
  • Learn to start thinking about functions in terms of inputs and outputs
  • Learn the return keyword
wiki_pages.manifest_title: Week 5 Overview - More Loops + Functions + Scope

wiki_pages.title: Week 6 Overview - Arrow functions and APIs
wiki_pages.content_html:
This week will:

  • Learn a new way to declare functions
  • Learn how to make an API call and get data
  • Keep practicing loops
  • Keep practicing rendering content to the screen
wiki_pages.manifest_title: Week 6 Overview - Arrow functions and APIs

wiki_pages.title: Week 7 Overview - Forms + APIs / Project
wiki_pages.content_html:
This week will:

  • Learn how to get information from a user and pass it into an API call
  • Practice by writing lots of code
  • Complete the PetFinder API project
